title: 埃及十字架之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\27-埃及十字架之谜-埃勒里奎因.txt
summary:
Ellery Queen explains the complex motives and methods of Andrew Van der Veer (also known as Andrew Gregg Trevor), who orchestrated a series of murders to claim an inheritance. Ellery reveals that Van beheaded Croscot to prevent the body from being identified and to mislead investigators into believing the victim was either Van himself or Cling. Van utilized a "T-junction" concept to create a pattern of irrationality, necessitating the beheading of Brad and Megal to maintain the illusion of a fanatic killer.

Regarding the final murder, Ellery and Escham examine footprints near a shabby hut. Ellery deduces that the killer's footprints entering the shack were deeper than those leaving because Van was carrying the body of Klein from a nearby cave. Van had intended to create the illusion that the limping Croscot had entered the cottage, but he failed to account for how the weight of the body would affect the footprint depth.

Ellery clarifies that Van's reappearance at the crime scene was a calculated move to claim a $5,000 inheritance from Breda's will, which required him to be legally alive and identified by Megal. Van also used a note to suggest he was being hunted, making his "return" appear as if he were a victim of a vengeful killer. Officer Quinn provides specific motives: Van killed Tom because Tom took a woman he loved, and he killed Megal because Megal had controlled and withheld family money from him.

Finally, Ellery explains that the appearance of the innocent Striker at various crime scenes was a result of Van's manipulation of Croscot's movements. Ellery concludes by deciding to write a book titled *The Mystery of the Egyptian Cross* to cover his investigation expenses.
